mainmatter.p: A reflexive graph is given by a type A together with a family of types \mathopen {} \left ( \cong _A \right ) \mathclose {} \colon A \to A \to \mathsf {Type} and an assignment 1_{ x } : x \cong x to each x,y: A. The reflexive graph is called univalent when for all x:A the type \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } x \cong _A y is a proposition, and so is contractible with centre \mathopen {} \left ( x, 1_{ x } \right ) \mathclose {}.
We shall refer to a univalent reflexive graph as a path object.
We will write \mathsf {PathObj} for the (large) type of path objects. In our notation, we will at times silently coerce a given path object to its underlying type of vertices.

frontmatter.title: Path objects and the univalence axiom

mainmatter.p: The intrinsic path object on a type A, written A ^{=}, is defined by setting x \cong _{ A ^{=} } y : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( x = y \right ) \mathclose {} and 1_{ x } : \equiv \mathsf {refl}.
mainmatter.block: Proof. That the reflexive graph defined above is univalent follows immediately by identification induction.

mainmatter.p: The reflexive graph structure on types given by equivalences is univalent. We shall write \mathsf {Type} to indicate the resulting (large) path object of types and equivalences.

frontmatter.title: The univalence principle for dependent functions

mainmatter.p: The reflexive graph structure on \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx} by homotopy and identity homotopies is univalent.

Given a dependent function f \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}, if each component Bx is contractible with centre fx then the dependent product \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx} is contractible with centre f.
mainmatter.block: Proof. This follows immediately from univalence for functions.

mainmatter.p: The dependent product of a family of propositions is a proposition.
mainmatter.block: Proof. Let B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type} be a family of propositions; fixing f \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}, it suffices to show any other g \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx may be identified with f. As B is a family of propositions, we know that each Bx is contractible with centre fx. Therefore, the closure of contractible types under dependent products shows that \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx is contractible with centre f.

mainmatter.p: Let B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} be a family of path objects. We equip the dependent product \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx} with the structure of a path object, setting f \cong _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}} g : \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } fx \cong _{Bx}gx and 1_{ f } : \equiv \lambda x \mathpunct {.} 1_{ fx }.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Univalence follows from the closure of families of contractible types under dependent products via the distributivity of \prod over \sum.

mainmatter.p: If f \colon A \to B has a quasi-inverse g \colon B \to A, then a path object structure on A induces a path object structure on B, setting \mathopen {} \left ( x \cong _B y \right ) \mathclose {} : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( gx \cong _A gy \right ) \mathclose {}.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We first prove the lemma for biinvertible functions, so that both the section and retraction are tracked by g. In this case, we may use singleton induction via the univalence principle for types to restrict attention to the case that f is the identity map, and the result is immediate.

mainmatter.p: Let A be a path object, and let B \colon A \to \mathsf {Prop} be a family of propositions. Then the dependent sum projection \pi _1 \colon \mathopen {} \left ( \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx \right ) \mathclose {} \to A creates a path object structure \mathopen {} \left \{ x:A \, \middle \vert \, Bx \right \} \mathclose {} on \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx as follows:

ol.li: we have \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x:A \, \middle \vert \, Bx \right \} \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ( y,v \right ) \mathclose {} definitionally equal to x \cong _A y;
the reflexivity datum 1_{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} } is given by 1_{ x } in A;
univalence follows from univalence of A and the fact that B is valued in propositions.

mainmatter.p: Let H \colon f \sim g be a homotopy between two functions f,g \colon A \to B. For any identification p \colon x =y in A, we have an identification H x \cdot \mathsf {ap}_{ g } \, p = \mathsf {ap}_{ f } \, p \cdot Hy.
mainmatter.block: Proof. By the univalence principle for functions, we may consider just the case that f \equiv g and H \equiv 1_{ f }, and so we must show that \mathsf {refl} \cdot \mathsf {ap}_{ g } p = \mathsf {ap}_{ f } p \cdot \mathsf {refl}. By identification induction, we may assume x \equiv y and p \equiv \mathsf {refl} to show \mathsf {refl} \cdot \mathsf {refl} = \mathsf {refl} \cdot \mathsf {refl}, which is immediate.

frontmatter.title: Coherence for homotopies onto the identity

mainmatter.p: Let H \colon f \sim 1_{ A } be a homotopy from f \colon A \to A onto the identity function. Then we may construct a higher homotopy from H \circ f to \mathsf {ap}_{ f } \circ H.
mainmatter.block: Proof. By the univalence principle for functions, we may consider only the case that f \equiv 1_{ A } and H \equiv 1_{ 1_{ A } } exhibit the identity homotopy 1_{ 1_{ 1_{ A } } } \colon 1_{ 1_{ A } } \sim 1_{ 1_{ A } }.

mainmatter.p: A type A is said to be contractible with centre a:A when every x:A is equipped with an identification \chi _x \colon a = x. We say that A is contractible when it comes equipped with some a:A such that A is contractible with centre a. We sometimes refer to a contractible type as a singleton.
A type A is said to be a proposition when every x:A, the type A is contractible with centre x, i.e. all elements of A are identified.

Let A be a path object, and let B \colon A \to \mathsf {Prop} be a family of propositions. Then the dependent sum projection \pi _1 \colon \mathopen {} \left ( \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx \right ) \mathclose {} \to A creates a path object structure \mathopen {} \left \{ x:A \, \middle \vert \, Bx \right \} \mathclose {} on \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx as follows:

ol.li: we have \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x:A \, \middle \vert \, Bx \right \} \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ( y,v \right ) \mathclose {} definitionally equal to x \cong _A y;
the reflexivity datum 1_{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} } is given by 1_{ x } in A;
univalence follows from univalence of A and the fact that B is valued in propositions.

frontmatter.title: Display of cocartesian families of path objects

mainmatter.p: Let A be a path object. Any cocartesian family of path objects B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} gives rise to a canonical displayed path object over A in which:

ol.li: for p \colon x \cong _A y, we define b \cong _B^p b' to be p_*b \cong _{By} b';

for x:A and b:Bx, the identity element 1_{ b } \colon b \cong _B^{ 1_{ x } }b is given by the component \Phi _Bxb \colon \mathopen {} \left ( 1_{ x } \right ) \mathclose {} _*b \cong _{Bx}b of the unit law of B.

mainmatter.block: Proof. Univalence is easily established using the univalence of each Bx

frontmatter.title: Display of cartesian families of path objects

mainmatter.p: Let A be a path object. Any cartesian family of path objects B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} gives rise to a canonical displayed path object over A in which:

ol.li: for p \colon x \cong _A y, we define b \cong _B^p b' to be b \cong _{By} p^*b';
for x:A and b:Bx, the identity element 1_{ b } \colon b \cong _B^{ 1_{ x } }b is given by the component \Phi _Bxb \colon b \cong _{Bx} 1_{ x } ^*b of the unit law of B.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Univalence is more difficult to establish than for the case of cocartesian families. Fixing a:A, we must show that the reflexive graph structure on B a with edges \mathopen {} \left ( b \cong _B^{ 1_{ a } } b' \right ) \mathclose {} \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( b \cong _{Ba} 1_{ a } ^*b \right ) \mathclose {} and reflexivity elements for b:Ba given by \Phi _B ab \colon b \cong _{Ba} 1_{ a } ^*b is univalent.

We proceed by identifying this reflexive graph structure with the assumed one on Ba using the univalence principle for reflexive graphs, and using the fact that the latter is assumed to be univalent. The desired equivalence of reflexive graphs is given by straightforward path algebra.

mainmatter.p: Let A be a path object; an A-indexed family B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} of path objects is called fibrant when it comes equipped with a cleaving as defined below:

ol.li: a restriction operation sending each p \colon x \cong _A y to some specified map p^* \colon By \to Bx;

a family \Phi _B \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } } b \cong _{Bx} 1_{ x } ^*b witnessing the unit law that restriction along the identity element 1_{ x } \colon x \cong _A x is the identity function.

mainmatter.p: Let A be a path object; an A-indexed family B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} of path objects is called a cocartesian family when it comes equipped with an opcleaving as defined below:

ol.li: a pushforward operation sending each p \colon x \cong _A y to some specified map p_* \colon Bx \to By;

a family \Phi _B \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ( 1_{ x } \right ) \mathclose {} _*b \cong _{Bx} b witnessing the unit law that pushforward along the identity element 1_{ x } \colon x \cong _A x is the identity function.

frontmatter.title: Contravariant sum of cartesian families of path objects

mainmatter.p: Let B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} be an cartesian family of path objects over a path object A. We define the contravariant sum of B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj}, written \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}, to be the total path object of the displayed path object associated to B. In particular, we have defined \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx} \mathopen {} \left ( y,v \right ) \mathclose {} to be \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } } u \cong _{Bx} p^*v.

frontmatter.title: Covariant sum of an cocartesian family of path objects

mainmatter.p: Let B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} be an cocartesian family of path objects over a path object A. We define the covariant sum of B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj}, written \mathchoice { \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}, to be the total path object of the displayed path object associated to B. In particular, we have defined \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}} \mathopen {} \left ( y,v \right ) \mathclose {} to be \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } } p_*u \cong _{By} v.

We have seen how to obtain principles for equational reasoning by imposing path object structures on types; in general, we will need an analogous tool for working with families of types B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type} given a path object A. Such a tool must account for heterogeneous identifications in B, i.e. identifications between elements of different fibers of B; in this section, we explore three possible variations on the concept of “dependent path object”:

ol.li: displayed path objects provide an unbiased and heterogeneous account of identifications between elements of different fibers of B;
cocartesian families of path objects reduce heterogeneous identification to homogeneous identification by means of a forward transport operation;
cartesian families of path objects reduce heterogeneous identification to homogeneous identification by means of a backward transport operation.

mainmatter.p: All of these notions are useful: because it is unbiased (i.e. prefers neither forward nor backward transport), the displayed representation is the most fundamental; on the other hand, the biased cartesian and cocartesian representations are the most frequently used. The fact that the latter are described directly for families of path objects in the existing sense is practically important for the compositionality of reasoning in univalent foundations, as we will more frequently have general-purpose building blocks for composing path objects than for composing displayed path objects.

Let A be a path object. A displayed reflexive graph over A is given by families of types B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type} and \mathopen {} \left ( \cong _B^ \bullet \right ) \mathclose {} \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x,y:A \right \} \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x,y:A \right \} \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x,y:A \right \} \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x,y:A \right \} \mathclose {} } } x \cong _A y \to Bx \to By \to \mathsf {Type} together with an assignment of displayed reflexivity data 1_{ u } \colon b \cong _B^{ 1_{ x } } b for every u:Bx. The displayed reflexive graph is called univalent when for each x:A the induced reflexive graph \mathopen {} \left ( \cong _{Bx} \right ) \mathclose {} : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( \cong ^{ 1_{ x } }_B \right ) \mathclose {} on Bx is univalent. We define a displayed path object to be a univalent displayed reflexive graph.

We will write \mathsf {DispPathObj} \, A for the (large) type of displayed path objects over A. We will at times silently coerce a displayed path object B \colon \mathsf {DispPathObj} \, A to its underlying family of vertices B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type}.
Let A be a path object; an A-indexed family B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} of path objects is called a cocartesian family when it comes equipped with an opcleaving as defined below:

ol.li: a pushforward operation sending each p \colon x \cong _A y to some specified map p_* \colon Bx \to By;

a family \Phi _B \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ( 1_{ x } \right ) \mathclose {} _*b \cong _{Bx} b witnessing the unit law that pushforward along the identity element 1_{ x } \colon x \cong _A x is the identity function.

mainmatter.p: Let A be a path object; an A-indexed family B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} of path objects is called fibrant when it comes equipped with a cleaving as defined below:

ol.li: a restriction operation sending each p \colon x \cong _A y to some specified map p^* \colon By \to Bx;

a family \Phi _B \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } } b \cong _{Bx} 1_{ x } ^*b witnessing the unit law that restriction along the identity element 1_{ x } \colon x \cong _A x is the identity function.

mainmatter.p: Our goal is to show that cartesian and cocartesian families of path objects both give rise to appropriate displayed path objects on their base. We will do this incrementally.

frontmatter.title: Display of cocartesian families of path objects

mainmatter.p: Let A be a path object. Any cocartesian family of path objects B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} gives rise to a canonical displayed path object over A in which:

ol.li: for p \colon x \cong _A y, we define b \cong _B^p b' to be p_*b \cong _{By} b';

for x:A and b:Bx, the identity element 1_{ b } \colon b \cong _B^{ 1_{ x } }b is given by the component \Phi _Bxb \colon \mathopen {} \left ( 1_{ x } \right ) \mathclose {} _*b \cong _{Bx}b of the unit law of B.

mainmatter.block: Proof. Univalence is easily established using the univalence of each Bx

frontmatter.title: The total path object of a displayed path object

mainmatter.p: Let A be a path object, and let B: \mathsf {DispPathObj} \, A be a displayed path object structure over A. The total path object of B over A, written A.B, is defined as follows:

Reflexivity data is given by assigning 1_{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} } : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( 1_{ x } , 1_{ u } \right ) \mathclose {}. Univalence is established in two singleton inductions, first using univalence of \mathopen {} \left ( \cong _A \right ) \mathclose {} followed by univalence of \mathopen {} \left ( \cong _B^ \bullet \right ) \mathclose {}.

frontmatter.title: Covariant sum of an cocartesian family of path objects

mainmatter.p: Let B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} be an cocartesian family of path objects over a path object A. We define the covariant sum of B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj}, written \mathchoice { \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}, to be the total path object of the displayed path object associated to B. In particular, we have defined \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}} \mathopen {} \left ( y,v \right ) \mathclose {} to be \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } } p_*u \cong _{By} v.

frontmatter.title: The univalence principle for reflexive graphs

mainmatter.p: We may define a path object structure on the type of reflexive graphs with vertices in a given type A, so that an isomorphism from \mathopen {} \left ( G, 1_{ \bullet } \right ) \mathclose {} to \mathopen {} \left ( G', 1_{ \bullet } ' \right ) \mathclose {} is given by a pair of a pointwise equivalence of types H \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Gxy \cong G'xy that preserves reflexivity data as witnessed by a dependent homotopy I \colon \mathopen {} \left ( \lambda x \mathpunct {.} H \, x \, x \, 1_{ x } \right ) \mathclose {} \sim 1_{ \bullet } '.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We first show that the dependent product of intrinsic path objects \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ( -xx \right ) \mathclose {} ^{=} is a cocartesian family of path objects over the path object \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type}: given an equivalence H : G \cong G' of graphs and a reflexivity datum 1_{ \bullet } \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Gxx, we define H_* 1_{ \bullet } x : \equiv H \, x \, x \, 1_{ x }; the unit law holds definitionally.

Therefore, we may form the covariant sum \mathchoice { \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( G: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( G: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( G: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( G: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ( Gxx \right ) \mathclose {} ^{=}, in which equivalences are given by homotopies of reflexivity data over graph equivalences.

frontmatter.title: Display of cartesian families of path objects

mainmatter.p: Let A be a path object. Any cartesian family of path objects B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} gives rise to a canonical displayed path object over A in which:

ol.li: for p \colon x \cong _A y, we define b \cong _B^p b' to be b \cong _{By} p^*b';
for x:A and b:Bx, the identity element 1_{ b } \colon b \cong _B^{ 1_{ x } }b is given by the component \Phi _Bxb \colon b \cong _{Bx} 1_{ x } ^*b of the unit law of B.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Univalence is more difficult to establish than for the case of cocartesian families. Fixing a:A, we must show that the reflexive graph structure on B a with edges \mathopen {} \left ( b \cong _B^{ 1_{ a } } b' \right ) \mathclose {} \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( b \cong _{Ba} 1_{ a } ^*b \right ) \mathclose {} and reflexivity elements for b:Ba given by \Phi _B ab \colon b \cong _{Ba} 1_{ a } ^*b is univalent.

We proceed by identifying this reflexive graph structure with the assumed one on Ba using the univalence principle for reflexive graphs, and using the fact that the latter is assumed to be univalent. The desired equivalence of reflexive graphs is given by straightforward path algebra.

frontmatter.title: Contravariant sum of cartesian families of path objects

mainmatter.p: Let B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} be an cartesian family of path objects over a path object A. We define the contravariant sum of B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj}, written \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}, to be the total path object of the displayed path object associated to B. In particular, we have defined \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx} \mathopen {} \left ( y,v \right ) \mathclose {} to be \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } } u \cong _{Bx} p^*v.

frontmatter.title: The intrinsic opcleaving of a family of path objects

mainmatter.p: Given a type A and a family of path objects B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj}, we can always define a opcleaving rendering B fibrant over the intrinsic path object on A. The opcleaving is obtained directly from the transport operation of the identification type.

Given f \colon A \to B and b:B, we shall impose a path object structure on the fiber \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } fx=b of f over b. The family \mathopen {} \left ( f-=b \right ) \mathclose {} ^{=} \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} given by intrinsic path objects in each component is a cartesian family over A ^{=}:

ol.li: for p \colon x = y and \phi :fy=b, we define p^*b \colon fx=b to be identification \mathsf {ap}_{ f } p \cdot \phi;
the restriction counit law can be seen to hold using the unit laws for composition of identification.

mainmatter.p: Therefore, we may compute the contravariant sum \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } } { \mathopen {} \left ( fx=b \right ) \mathclose {} ^{=} } using the cleaving above, with \mathopen {} \left ( x, \phi \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } } { \mathopen {} \left ( fx=b \right ) \mathclose {} ^{=} }} \mathopen {} \left ( y, \psi \right ) \mathclose {} definitionally equal to \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p \colon x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p \colon x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p \colon x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p \colon x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } } \phi = \mathsf {ap}_{ f } p \cdot \psi.

frontmatter.title: The standard cleaving for sectionhood witnesses

mainmatter.p: Let f \colon A \to B be a given function. Given a homotopy H \colon s \sim s' \colon B \to A and a witness h \colon f \circ s' \sim 1_{ B } that s' is a section of f, we may construct a witness H^*h \colon f \circ s \sim 1_{ B } that s is a section of f as follows:
mainmatter.tex: H^*h x : \equiv \mathsf {ap}_{ f } \mathopen {} \left ( Hx \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h x
mainmatter.p: As we evidently have 1_{ s } ^*h x = \mathsf {ap}_{ f } \mathsf {refl} \cdot h x = h x, the data above constitute a cleaving of f \circ- \sim 1_{ B } over the path object B \to A ^{=}, both with their standard path object structure as given by (dependent) homotopies. In this sense, sectionhood witnesses constitute a cartesian family of path objects.

frontmatter.title: The standard cleaving for retractionhood witnesses

mainmatter.p: Let f \colon A \to B be a given function. Given a homotopy H \colon r \sim r' \colon B \to A and a witness h \colon r' \circ f \sim 1_{ A } that r' is a retraction of f, we may construct a witness H^*h \colon r \circ f \sim 1_{ A } that r is a retraction of f as follows:
mainmatter.tex: H^*h x : \equiv H \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h x
mainmatter.p: As we have 1_{ r } ^*h x = h x, the data above constitute a cleaving of - \circ f \sim 1_{ B } over B \to A ^{=}, both with their standard path object structure as given by (dependent) homotopies. Thus retractionhood witnesses constitute a cartesian family of path objects.

A suitable path object on sections to a fixed function r \colon A \to B is given by a contravariant sum of path objects \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( s:B \to A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( s:B \to A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( s:B \to A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( s:B \to A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } } r \circ s \sim 1_{ B }, as sectionhood witnesses are fibrant over their underlying sections. In particular, an isomorphism of sections of r from \mathopen {} \left ( s,h \right ) \mathclose {} to \mathopen {} \left ( s',h' \right ) \mathclose {} is given by a homotopy H_s \colon s \sim s' together with a higher homotopy H_h \colon h \sim \mathopen {} \left ( \lambda x \mathpunct {.} \mathsf {ap}_{ r } \mathopen {} \left ( H_s x \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h'x \right ) \mathclose {}.

A suitable path object on retractions to a fixed function s \colon B \to A is given by a contravariant sum of path objects \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( r:A \to B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( r:A \to B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( r:A \to B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( r:A \to B \right ) \mathclose {} } } r \circ s \sim 1_{ B }, as retractionhood witnesses are fibrant over their underlying sections. In particular, an isomorphism of retractions of s from \mathopen {} \left ( r,h \right ) \mathclose {} to \mathopen {} \left ( r',h' \right ) \mathclose {} is given by a homotopy H_r \colon r \sim r' together with a higher homotopy H_h \colon h \sim \mathopen {} \left ( \lambda x \mathpunct {.} H_r \mathopen {} \left ( r x \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h'x \right ) \mathclose {}.

frontmatter.title: The univalence principle for reflexive graphs

mainmatter.p: We may define a path object structure on the type of reflexive graphs with vertices in a given type A, so that an isomorphism from \mathopen {} \left ( G, 1_{ \bullet } \right ) \mathclose {} to \mathopen {} \left ( G', 1_{ \bullet } ' \right ) \mathclose {} is given by a pair of a pointwise equivalence of types H \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Gxy \cong G'xy that preserves reflexivity data as witnessed by a dependent homotopy I \colon \mathopen {} \left ( \lambda x \mathpunct {.} H \, x \, x \, 1_{ x } \right ) \mathclose {} \sim 1_{ \bullet } '.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We first show that the dependent product of intrinsic path objects \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ( -xx \right ) \mathclose {} ^{=} is a cocartesian family of path objects over the path object \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type}: given an equivalence H : G \cong G' of graphs and a reflexivity datum 1_{ \bullet } \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Gxx, we define H_* 1_{ \bullet } x : \equiv H \, x \, x \, 1_{ x }; the unit law holds definitionally.

Therefore, we may form the covariant sum \mathchoice { \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( G: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( G: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( G: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( G: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ( Gxx \right ) \mathclose {} ^{=}, in which equivalences are given by homotopies of reflexivity data over graph equivalences.

mainmatter.p: A suitable path object on sections to a fixed function r \colon A \to B is given by a contravariant sum of path objects \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( s:B \to A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( s:B \to A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( s:B \to A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( s:B \to A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } } r \circ s \sim 1_{ B }, as sectionhood witnesses are fibrant over their underlying sections. In particular, an isomorphism of sections of r from \mathopen {} \left ( s,h \right ) \mathclose {} to \mathopen {} \left ( s',h' \right ) \mathclose {} is given by a homotopy H_s \colon s \sim s' together with a higher homotopy H_h \colon h \sim \mathopen {} \left ( \lambda x \mathpunct {.} \mathsf {ap}_{ r } \mathopen {} \left ( H_s x \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h'x \right ) \mathclose {}.

A suitable path object on retractions to a fixed function s \colon B \to A is given by a contravariant sum of path objects \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( r:A \to B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( r:A \to B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( r:A \to B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( r:A \to B \right ) \mathclose {} } } r \circ s \sim 1_{ B }, as retractionhood witnesses are fibrant over their underlying sections. In particular, an isomorphism of retractions of s from \mathopen {} \left ( r,h \right ) \mathclose {} to \mathopen {} \left ( r',h' \right ) \mathclose {} is given by a homotopy H_r \colon r \sim r' together with a higher homotopy H_h \colon h \sim \mathopen {} \left ( \lambda x \mathpunct {.} H_r \mathopen {} \left ( r x \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h'x \right ) \mathclose {}.

frontmatter.title: The standard cleaving for sectionhood witnesses

mainmatter.p: Let f \colon A \to B be a given function. Given a homotopy H \colon s \sim s' \colon B \to A and a witness h \colon f \circ s' \sim 1_{ B } that s' is a section of f, we may construct a witness H^*h \colon f \circ s \sim 1_{ B } that s is a section of f as follows:
mainmatter.tex: H^*h x : \equiv \mathsf {ap}_{ f } \mathopen {} \left ( Hx \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h x
mainmatter.p: As we evidently have 1_{ s } ^*h x = \mathsf {ap}_{ f } \mathsf {refl} \cdot h x = h x, the data above constitute a cleaving of f \circ- \sim 1_{ B } over the path object B \to A ^{=}, both with their standard path object structure as given by (dependent) homotopies. In this sense, sectionhood witnesses constitute a cartesian family of path objects.

frontmatter.title: The standard cleaving for retractionhood witnesses

mainmatter.p: Let f \colon A \to B be a given function. Given a homotopy H \colon r \sim r' \colon B \to A and a witness h \colon r' \circ f \sim 1_{ A } that r' is a retraction of f, we may construct a witness H^*h \colon r \circ f \sim 1_{ A } that r is a retraction of f as follows:
mainmatter.tex: H^*h x : \equiv H \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h x
mainmatter.p: As we have 1_{ r } ^*h x = h x, the data above constitute a cleaving of - \circ f \sim 1_{ B } over B \to A ^{=}, both with their standard path object structure as given by (dependent) homotopies. Thus retractionhood witnesses constitute a cartesian family of path objects.

Let B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} be a family of path objects. We equip the dependent product \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx} with the structure of a path object, setting f \cong _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}} g : \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } fx \cong _{Bx}gx and 1_{ f } : \equiv \lambda x \mathpunct {.} 1_{ fx }.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Univalence follows from the closure of families of contractible types under dependent products via the distributivity of \prod over \sum.

mainmatter.p: If f \colon A \to B has a quasi-inverse g \colon B \to A, then a path object structure on A induces a path object structure on B, setting \mathopen {} \left ( x \cong _B y \right ) \mathclose {} : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( gx \cong _A gy \right ) \mathclose {}.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We first prove the lemma for biinvertible functions, so that both the section and retraction are tracked by g. In this case, we may use singleton induction via the univalence principle for types to restrict attention to the case that f is the identity map, and the result is immediate.

mainmatter.p: Given f \colon A \to B and b:B, we shall impose a path object structure on the fiber \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } fx=b of f over b. The family \mathopen {} \left ( f-=b \right ) \mathclose {} ^{=} \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} given by intrinsic path objects in each component is a cartesian family over A ^{=}:

ol.li: for p \colon x = y and \phi :fy=b, we define p^*b \colon fx=b to be identification \mathsf {ap}_{ f } p \cdot \phi;
the restriction counit law can be seen to hold using the unit laws for composition of identification.

mainmatter.p: Therefore, we may compute the contravariant sum \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } } { \mathopen {} \left ( fx=b \right ) \mathclose {} ^{=} } using the cleaving above, with \mathopen {} \left ( x, \phi \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } } { \mathopen {} \left ( fx=b \right ) \mathclose {} ^{=} }} \mathopen {} \left ( y, \psi \right ) \mathclose {} definitionally equal to \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p \colon x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p \colon x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p \colon x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p \colon x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } } \phi = \mathsf {ap}_{ f } p \cdot \psi.
The reflexive graph structure on types given by equivalences is univalent. We shall write \mathsf {Type} to indicate the resulting (large) path object of types and equivalences.

Let A be a path object. A displayed reflexive graph over A is given by families of types B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type} and \mathopen {} \left ( \cong _B^ \bullet \right ) \mathclose {} \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x,y:A \right \} \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x,y:A \right \} \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x,y:A \right \} \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x,y:A \right \} \mathclose {} } } x \cong _A y \to Bx \to By \to \mathsf {Type} together with an assignment of displayed reflexivity data 1_{ u } \colon b \cong _B^{ 1_{ x } } b for every u:Bx. The displayed reflexive graph is called univalent when for each x:A the induced reflexive graph \mathopen {} \left ( \cong _{Bx} \right ) \mathclose {} : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( \cong ^{ 1_{ x } }_B \right ) \mathclose {} on Bx is univalent. We define a displayed path object to be a univalent displayed reflexive graph.

We will write \mathsf {DispPathObj} \, A for the (large) type of displayed path objects over A. We will at times silently coerce a displayed path object B \colon \mathsf {DispPathObj} \, A to its underlying family of vertices B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type}.
The intrinsic path object on a type A, written A ^{=}, is defined by setting x \cong _{ A ^{=} } y : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( x = y \right ) \mathclose {} and 1_{ x } : \equiv \mathsf {refl}.
mainmatter.block: Proof. That the reflexive graph defined above is univalent follows immediately by identification induction.

frontmatter.title: The intrinsic opcleaving of a family of path objects

mainmatter.p: Given a type A and a family of path objects B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj}, we can always define a opcleaving rendering B fibrant over the intrinsic path object on A. The opcleaving is obtained directly from the transport operation of the identification type.

frontmatter.title: The total path object of a displayed path object

mainmatter.p: Let A be a path object, and let B: \mathsf {DispPathObj} \, A be a displayed path object structure over A. The total path object of B over A, written A.B, is defined as follows:

Reflexivity data is given by assigning 1_{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} } : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( 1_{ x } , 1_{ u } \right ) \mathclose {}. Univalence is established in two singleton inductions, first using univalence of \mathopen {} \left ( \cong _A \right ) \mathclose {} followed by univalence of \mathopen {} \left ( \cong _B^ \bullet \right ) \mathclose {}.

frontmatter.title: The univalence principle for dependent functions

mainmatter.p: The reflexive graph structure on \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx} by homotopy and identity homotopies is univalent.
